children.name: Archilochus
children.description: Bee hummingbirds — tiny, iridescent hummingbirds of North and Central America. Archilochus species are among the smallest birds on Earth, weighing just 3-4 grams but undertaking extraordinary migrations across the Gulf of Mexico. Their wings beat up to 80 times per second, producing the characteristic humming sound that gives the family its name.

children.name: Archilochus colubris
children.description: The only hummingbird breeding in eastern North America. Iridescent green above, white below, with a brilliant ruby-red throat in males. Remarkably, it migrates non-stop across the Gulf of Mexico — 800 km in one flight, impossible for any other land bird of its size.

children.name: Archilochus alexandri
children.description: Widespread across western North America. Males have a velvety black chin bordered by an iridescent purple band, visible only in good light. Highly adaptable, found from desert canyons to suburban gardens. Winters in Mexico.

children.name: Calypte
children.description: Small to medium-sized hummingbirds of North America. Anna's Hummingbird is the only hummingbird resident year-round in the Pacific Northwest, surviving cold winters by entering torpor on freezing nights.

children.name: Calypte anna
children.description: A stunning hummingbird of the Pacific coast. Males have a rose-red crown and throat that flash brilliant magenta in sunlight. Unlike most temperate hummingbirds, it does not migrate south; instead, it survives mild Pacific winters by entering nightly torpor. Named after Anna Massena, Duchess of Rivoli.

children.name: Selasphorus
children.description: Small, aggressive hummingbirds of western North America. Rufous Hummingbirds are notoriously territorial, chasing birds many times their size. Despite weighing only 3 grams, they undertake one of the longest migrations of any hummingbird — from Alaska to Mexico.

children.name: Selasphorus rufus
children.description: Brilliant rufous-orange body with an iridescent red throat. Breeds from southeastern Alaska to Oregon, wintering in Mexico. One of the feistiest hummingbirds, known to chase away birds and even squirrels from feeders. Its migration route is over 4,000 km one way.

children.name: Phaethornis
children.description: Hermit hummingbirds — a large group of tropical hummingbirds with long, decurved bills and elongated central tail feathers. Unlike most hummingbirds, hermits are duller in colour (browns, greys, and greens) and do not gather at leks. They are specialised for feeding on flowers with long, curved corollas, and are important pollinators of tropical plants.

children.name: Phaethornis longirostris
children.description: A large hermit with a strikingly long, decurved bill, found from southern Mexico to Ecuador. Brownish-green above, pale below with a dark mask. Its song is a series of high-pitched notes. Pollinates heliconias and other long-tubed flowers.

children.name: Phaethornis superciliosus
children.description: Found across the Amazon Basin and the Guianas. Distinguished by its long, graduated tail and prominent white supercilium. Feeds by trap-lining — visiting a regular circuit of flowers in the understory of lowland rainforest.

children.name: Patagona
children.description: The giant hummingbirds — the largest hummingbirds in the world, reaching 23 cm in length and weighing up to 24 grams. Despite their name, they are still tiny compared to most birds. Found in the high Andes, they hover like all hummingbirds but with a slower, more deliberate wingbeat.

children.name: Patagona gigas
children.description: The largest hummingbird, measuring up to 23 cm. Found in the Andes from Ecuador to Argentina and Chile. Drab brownish-grey plumage with a rufous belly — surprisingly inconspicuous for such a record-breaker. Its wingbeat is slow enough to be visible, about 15 beats per second.

children.name: Topaza
children.description: Topaz hummingbirds — two of the most spectacular hummingbirds in the Neotropics, found in the Amazon Basin. Males have iridescent crimson, golden, and green plumage that makes them look like living jewels. Their long, crossed tail feathers are unique among birds.

children.name: Topaza pella
children.description: A breathtaking hummingbird of the Amazon and Guianan shields. Males have a glowing crimson body, golden-green throat, and black wings. The long, crossed central tail feathers are unique. Inhabits lowland rainforest near streams and rivers.

children.name: Lampornis
children.description: Mountain-gems — robust, medium-sized hummingbirds of highland forests from Mexico to Panama. Named for their brilliant throat colours that flash like gemstones in the sun. They inhabit cloud forests and high-elevation oak woodlands.

children.name: Lampornis clemenciae
children.description: The largest hummingbird in North America after the Giant Hummingbird. Iridescent green above, grey below, with a brilliant blue throat. Found in pine-oak woodlands of Mexico and the southwestern United States. Its loud, high-pitched song is one of the most complex of any hummingbird.

children.name: Lampornis amethystinus
children.description: Found in highland forests from Mexico to Honduras. Males have a brilliant amethyst-purple throat and a white postocular stripe. Females lack the throat colour. Feeds on flowers in the mid-story and canopy of cloud forests.

children.name: Oreotrochilus
children.description: Hillstars — high-altitude hummingbirds of the Andean páramo, found at elevations up to 5,200 metres. They tolerate extreme cold by entering deep torpor and roosting in sheltered rock crevices. Their stout bodies and short, straight bills are adapted for feeding on high-altitude flowers.

children.name: Oreotrochilus estella
children.description: A high-altitude specialist found in the Andes from Peru to Chile and Argentina. Greyish-brown above, white below with a rufous belly and a brilliant green or blue throat. Survives at altitudes where night temperatures regularly fall below freezing.

children.name: Ensifera
children.description: The sword-billed hummingbird — a monotypic genus with the most extreme bill of any bird. The bill is longer than the body (up to 12 cm) and is used to feed from flowers with long corollas that no other bird can access. The bird cannot preen with its bill and must use its feet.

children.name: Ensifera ensifera
children.description: The only bird with a bill longer than its body. The sword-like bill reaches 12 cm, while the body is only 10-12 cm. Found in the Andes from Venezuela to Bolivia. Co-evolved with passionflowers and other deep-tubed blooms. When perched, the bird holds its bill at a 45-degree angle to keep its balance.

children.name: Aglaiocercus
children.description: Sylphs — hummingbirds with extraordinarily long, forked tails that can reach twice the body length. The males' tails are adorned with iridescent colours and are used in spectacular aerial courtship displays.

children.name: Aglaiocercus kingii
children.description: A spectacular hummingbird with a tail that reaches 15 cm — longer than the rest of the body. Iridescent green body, blue throat, and extraordinary blue-violet tail streamers. Found in cloud forests of the Andes from Venezuela to Bolivia.

children.name: Loddigesia
children.description: The marvellous spatuletail — a monotypic genus and one of the most extraordinary hummingbirds in the world. The male has only four tail feathers, two of which are elongated bare shafts ending in broad, violet-blue discs or 'spatules' that move independently and are used in a mesmerising courtship dance.

children.name: Loddigesia mirabilis
children.description: One of the most remarkable birds on Earth. The male has just four tail feathers: the two outer ones are bare shafts tipped with iridescent violet-blue discs that buzz and swing during his courtship display. Endemic to a small area of the Peruvian Andes. Classified as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Amazilia
children.description: Emerald hummingbirds — a large genus of medium-sized hummingbirds found from Mexico through Central and South America. Most species are predominantly green, with vivid throat colours ranging from emerald to ruby. They are common garden visitors throughout their range.

children.name: Amazilia amazilia
children.description: Found in arid and semi-arid regions of coastal Peru and Ecuador. Green crown and back, white belly, and a distinctive rufous belly band. Adaptable to gardens and urban parks. Named after the Inca heroine Amazilia.

children.name: Amazilia tzacatl
children.description: One of the most common and widespread hummingbirds from Mexico to Ecuador. Bronze-green above, pale below, with a distinctive rufous tail that is constantly fanned. A regular visitor to gardens and feeders throughout its range.

children.name: Eulampis
children.description: Carib hummingbirds — two species endemic to the Caribbean islands. They are distinguished by brilliant iridescent throats and a highly curved bill adapted for feeding on heliconias and other tropical flowers. Important pollinators of Caribbean island ecosystems.

children.name: Eulampis jugularis
children.description: A large, dark hummingbird endemic to the Lesser Antilles. Males are iridescent purple and green with a brilliant amethyst throat. The bill is strongly curved, specialised for feeding on heliconia flowers. The female uses her longer bill to access deeper nectar sources.

children.name: Anthracothorax
children.description: Mango hummingbirds — medium to large hummingbirds found in the Neotropics. Males have velvety black or green plumage with brilliant metallic throats ranging from emerald green to amethyst. They are powerful fliers with broad wings.

children.name: Anthracothorax nigricollis
children.description: A widespread South American hummingbird found from Panama to northern Argentina and Brazil. Glossy green upperparts, white underparts, and a distinctive black throat stripe bordered by iridescent green. Found in open habitats, gardens, and forest edges.
